phrasalVerbs.definition: To accidentally hit against something; to meet someone unexpectedly.
examples: I bumped into an old friend at the supermarket.
Be careful not to bump into the glass door.
She bumped into her ex-boyfriend at the party.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To move to a particular place at the same time as a lot of other people.
examples: Fans crowded around the singer after the concert.
People crowded around the window to see what was happening.
Children crowded around the ice cream truck.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To move more slowly than other people so that you are behind them.
examples: She fell behind the rest of the group during the hike.
Don't fall behind—we need to stay together!
He fell behind in the race and couldn't catch up.

phrasalVerbs.definition: If a train, plane, etc. gets into a place, it arrives there.
examples: What time does the train get into London?
The flight gets into Paris at midnight.
We got into the station just as it started raining.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To change your position in order to make space for someone or something.
examples: Can you move over so I can sit down?
He moved over to let her through.
Move over a bit—I can't see the screen.

fillInExercises.sentence: I ___ ___ my old teacher at the shopping centre yesterday.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Bump into' means to meet someone unexpectedly.

fillInExercises.sentence: Fans ___ around the celebrity asking for autographs.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Crowd around' means to gather closely around someone or something.

fillInExercises.sentence: Don't ___ ___! We need to stay with the group.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Fall behind' means to move slower than others and end up behind them.

fillInExercises.sentence: What time does the plane ___ into New York?
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Get into' refers to when transport arrives at a destination.

fillInExercises.sentence: Could you ___ ___ a bit? I need more space.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Move over' means to change position to make room for someone.

fillInExercises.sentence: I ___ into the table and spilled my coffee.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Bump into' can also mean to accidentally hit something.

fillInExercises.sentence: The bus ___ ___ the station at 3 p.m.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Get into' means to arrive at a destination.

fillInExercises.sentence: She started to ___ behind during the long walk.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Fall behind' means to not keep up with others.

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.text: What time does your train [get into] Manchester?

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: It should [get into] the station around 4 p.m.

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.text: Can you [move over] so I can see the departure board?

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: Sure! Oh look, people are [crowding around] that platform.

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.text: Probably a delay. Try not to [fall behind] when we run for the train.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: I won't! Oh, I just [bumped into] my old colleague over there!

lines.speaker: Chen
lines.text: This concert is so crowded! People are [crowding around] the stage.

lines.speaker: Fatima
lines.text: Can we [move over] to get a better view?

lines.speaker: Chen
lines.text: Let's try. Just don't [fall behind]—I don't want to lose you.

lines.speaker: Fatima
lines.text: Okay. Oh no, I just [bumped into] someone! Sorry!

lines.speaker: Chen
lines.text: Be careful! What time does the last bus [get into] our area?

lines.speaker: Fatima
lines.text: Around midnight. We should leave before the crowd [bumps into] us on the way out.
